metadata.title: Guideline on the evaluation of medicinal products indicated for treatment of bacterial infections

rules.rule_text: Every effort should be made to elucidate the mechanism of action of new antibacterial agents.
rules.verbatim_quote: Every effort should be made to elucidate the mechanism of action of new antibacterial agents.

rules.rule_text: MIC determination methods must be described in detail and justified, with MIC50, MIC90, and MIC range presented by species and sub-group, including resistance mechanisms. MIC distributions must be presented in tables and histograms.
rules.verbatim_quote: The methods used for determination of minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) should be described in detail and justified. Appropriate active controls should be included. The MIC50, MIC90 and MIC range should be presented by species and, when appropriate, by sub-group (e.g. with and without specific resistance mechanisms) in tabular form. The MIC distributions should be presented in tables and in histograms.

rules.rule_text: In-vitro activity must be determined against clinical isolates obtained within 5 years prior to filing, sourced from various countries including a representative EU sample. For commonly encountered pathogens, several hundred isolates of each species should be tested.
rules.verbatim_quote: The in-vitro activity of previously unlicensed antibacterial agents and of combinations of beta-lactams and beta-lactamase inhibitors (BL/BLIs; see further in section 4.1.3) should be determined against clinical isolates obtained within 5 years prior to filing an application dossier. These isolates should belong to pathogenic species relevant to the indication(s) sought and should be sourced from various countries and regions, including a representative sample from within the EU. For commonly encountered pathogens it should be possible to test several hundred isolates of each species
rules.exceptions: For rare pathogens and strains with rarely encountered resistance, at least 10 organisms of each species or with each resistance mechanism/pattern are recommended.

rules.rule_text: The in-vitro antibacterial activity of any major metabolites formed in humans must be assessed separately. If a metabolite contributes to efficacy, in-vitro studies equivalent to the parent drug must be conducted.
rules.verbatim_quote: The in-vitro antibacterial activity of any major metabolites formed in humans should be assessed separately. If any metabolite appears to exert antibacterial activity that could make an important contribution to efficacy, an appropriate range of in-vitro studies should be conducted as would be done for parent drug.

rules.rule_text: For combination antibacterial agents, in-vitro susceptibility data must provide sound support for use of the combination compared to each agent alone against specific pathogens and/or organisms expressing certain mechanisms of resistance.
rules.verbatim_quote: The in-vitro susceptibility data should provide sound support for the use of the combination compared to each agent alone against specific pathogens and/or against organisms that express certain mechanisms of resistance.

rules.rule_text: The mechanism of beta-lactamase inhibition must be investigated for new BLIs using a wide range of beta-lactamases to determine the expected spectrum of inhibition. Studies must document whether the BLI itself has any antibacterial activity at clinically achievable plasma exposures.
rules.verbatim_quote: The mechanism of beta-lactamase inhibition should be investigated for new beta-lactamase inhibitors (BLIs) and enzyme kinetics should be investigated using a wide range of beta-lactamases to determine the expected spectrum of inhibition. The in-vitro studies should document whether the BLI itself has any antibacterial activity at clinically achievable plasma exposures.

rules.rule_text: For new class antibacterial agents, in-vitro susceptibility studies must assess the potential for cross-resistance with licensed agents of other classes, including strains demonstrating multi-drug and/or multi-class resistance.
rules.verbatim_quote: For test antibacterial agents of a new class, in-vitro susceptibility studies should assess the potential for cross-resistance to occur between the test agents and licensed agents of other classes. These studies should include strains (any of clinical isolates, laboratory strains or genetically engineered organisms) that express specific resistance mechanisms) that demonstrate multi-drug and/or multi-class resistance

rules.rule_text: Post-approval, any information on emerging resistance, changing patterns, or new mechanisms of resistance to the antibacterial agent must be notified promptly to EU regulators with a discussion of possible implications.
rules.verbatim_quote: After approval, any information that becomes available to the Marketing Authorisation Holder on emerging resistance, changing patterns of resistance or new mechanisms of resistance to the antibacterial agent should be notified promptly to EU regulators with a discussion of the possible implications

rules.rule_text: Minimum bactericidal concentrations (MBC) should be determined and time-kill studies should be conducted with relevant species and/or organisms expressing specific mechanisms of resistance.
rules.verbatim_quote: Minimum bactericidal concentrations (MBC) should be determined and time-kill studies should be conducted with relevant species and/or with organisms that express specific mechanisms of resistance.

rules.rule_text: The application dossier must include a justification for proposed interpretive criteria for susceptibility testing, with reference to PK-PD analyses used for dose regimen selection. Criteria must be finalised before an opinion is reached on the application.
rules.verbatim_quote: The application dossier should include a justification for the proposed interpretive criteria, which should include reference to the PK-PD analyses used to select the dose regimen(s)... it is expected that the criteria will be finalised before an opinion is reached on the application.
rules.exceptions: Not expected for agents with only local antibacterial action (topical, inhalation, oral GI-only).

rules.rule_text: Patient selection criteria must maximize the likelihood that patients have the type of bacterial infection under study and minimize enrolment of patients with infections likely to resolve rapidly without antibacterial therapy.
rules.verbatim_quote: The patient selection criteria should maximize the likelihood that patients have the type of bacterial infection under study and minimize enrolment of patients with infections that are likely to resolve rapidly without antibacterial therapy.

rules.rule_text: Patients must be categorised at baseline according to extent and/or severity of infection using available and widely recommended scoring schemes. Stratification at randomisation by disease factors known to strongly influence outcomes should be considered.
rules.source_section: 5.1.1 Clinical evidence of infection at enrolment
rules.verbatim_quote: It is recommended that patients are categorised at baseline according to the extent and/or severity of the infection to be treated using any available and widely recommended scoring schemes. Consideration should be given to stratification at randomisation by disease factors known to be very important for influencing outcomes.

rules.rule_text: If imaging studies are used for patient selection by trial site staff, a retrospective review by a panel of independent experts who are blinded to treatment assignment must determine patient eligibility for pre-defined analysis populations.
rules.source_section: 5.1.1 Clinical evidence of infection at enrolment
rules.verbatim_quote: If specialised or experimental imaging studies are used for patient selection based on interpretation by trial site staff, it is recommended that there is a retrospective review by a panel of independent experts who are unaware of treatment assignment and whose readings are used to determine patient eligibility for pre-defined analysis populations.

rules.rule_text: Microbiological methods used should be aligned across all trial sites. Central laboratory results should be used to define patient subsets for analyses of outcomes by pathogen.
rules.source_section: 5.1.2 Microbiological evidence of infection at enrolment
rules.verbatim_quote: The microbiological methods used should be aligned across all trial sites whenever possible and/or results obtained at central laboratories should be used to define patient subsets for analyses of outcomes by pathogen.

rules.rule_text: The same RDTs must be used at all trial sites to avoid potential imbalances in sensitivity/specificity affecting patient populations and outcomes.
rules.source_section: 5.1.2 Microbiological evidence of infection at enrolment
rules.verbatim_quote: Due to variations in the sensitivity and/or specificity of tests, it is recommended that the same RDTs are used at all trial sites to avoid the potential that:

rules.rule_text: Prior antibacterial therapy must be limited: usually no more than 24 hours of a potentially active regimen should be allowed before enrolment, except for patients who clearly failed prior treatment. A limit of no more than 30% of enrolled patients with prior therapy should be set.
rules.verbatim_quote: Usually, except for patients who clearly failed to respond to any prior treatment, no more than 24 hours of a potentially active antibacterial regimen, including any peri-operative or per-procedural prophylaxis, should be allowed prior to enrolment... In other cases, a limit (e.g. no more than 30% of the total enrolled; after excluding any patients who clearly failed prior treatment) should be set on the proportion who received prior potentially active antibacterial treatment.
rules.exceptions: Patients who clearly failed to respond to prior treatment are excepted from the 24-hour limit.

rules.rule_text: Specimens for culture or serology must be obtained at baseline from all patients. Specimens should be collected within a pre-defined window around randomisation, not exceeding 24 hours before or 12 hours after the first dose.
rules.verbatim_quote: Appropriate specimens for performing RDTs, culture or serology should be obtained at baseline from all patients (i.e. even if culture results are available from earlier samples)... they should be collected within a pre-defined window around the time of randomisation, which should not usually exceed 24 hours before or 12 hours after the first dose of assigned treatment.

rules.rule_text: Isolates must be shipped to designated central laboratories for confirmation of identity, susceptibility testing including MIC determination, and investigation of resistance mechanisms. Central labs must perform typing of baseline and post-baseline isolates to differentiate persistent infections, relapses, and new infections.
rules.source_section: 5.2.2 Confirmation of causative pathogens by culture
rules.verbatim_quote: Isolates should be shipped to designated central laboratories for confirmation of isolate identity and susceptibility testing, including determination of MICs of the test antibacterial agent and investigation of possible resistance mechanisms. Central laboratories with appropriate expertise should perform any typing of baseline and post-baseline isolates that is required to differentiate persistent infections and relapses from new infections with the same species.
rules.exceptions: If central laboratory results are missing because organisms did not survive shipping or cultures were contaminated, available local laboratory results may be used instead.

rules.rule_text: Protocols must list the pathogens that may be considered causative in the type of infection under study. Only patients with at least one baseline pathogen on the list should be included in the microbiological-ITT and microbiologically evaluable populations.
rules.verbatim_quote: Protocols should list the pathogens that may be considered causative in the type of infection under study. Only those patients with at least one baseline pathogen on the list should be included in the microbiological-ITT and microbiologically evaluable populations

rules.rule_text: Clinical dose-finding trials are not required if PK-PD analyses can provide adequate support for the dose regimen selected for pivotal efficacy trials.
rules.source_section: 5.3.1 Selection of the test antibacterial dose regimen
rules.verbatim_quote: In accordance with the Guideline on the use of pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics in the development of antimicrobial medicinal products (EMA/456046/2015), clinical dose-finding trials are not required if it is considered that the PK-PD analyses can provide adequate support for the dose regimen(s) selected for pivotal efficacy trials.
rules.exceptions: For formulations exerting local effect (topical, inhalational, intra-gut), dose-finding clinical trials should be conducted as PK-PD is not applicable.

rules.rule_text: If parenteral therapy is switched to oral, and there is no oral formulation of the test agent, parenteral therapy with the test antibacterial agent must be given for at least 5 days regardless of infection type. The oral follow-on agent should be of the same class whenever possible.
rules.source_section: 5.3.2 Switch from parenteral to oral therapy
rules.verbatim_quote: If there is no oral presentation of the test antibacterial agent, it is recommended that trials do not allow a switch to a licensed oral follow-on therapy. If allowing a switch is considered essential for trial feasibility reasons it is recommended that parenteral therapy with the test antibacterial agent is given for at least 5 days regardless of the type of infection under study. The oral follow-on agent should be of the same class as the test agent whenever possible.

rules.rule_text: If co-administration of additional antibacterial agents is needed, any additional agent must have a spectrum that does not overlap or minimally overlaps with the test antibacterial agent.
rules.source_section: 5.3.3 Co-administration of the test antibacterial agent with licensed agents
rules.verbatim_quote: Any additional agent should have a spectrum that does not overlap or minimally overlaps with that of the test antibacterial agent (e.g. it should cover only Gram-positive organisms if the test agent covers only Gram-negative organisms).
rules.exceptions: It may sometimes be necessary to add an agent that overlaps in spectrum (e.g. to cover P. aeruginosa), in which case monotherapy efficacy should be assessed in another infection type.

rules.rule_text: A non-inferiority trial design is acceptable only when there is a licensed treatment for the infection under study for which the magnitude of the treatment effect over placebo is known or can be estimated from existing data.
rules.verbatim_quote: A non-inferiority trial design is acceptable when there is a licensed treatment for the infection under study for which the magnitude of the treatment effect over placebo is known or can be estimated from existing data.
rules.exceptions: Alternative non-inferiority margins may be acceptable if adequately justified (e.g. based on pharmacometrics).

rules.rule_text: The comparative regimen in non-inferiority trials should be one of the best available treatments based on clinical trials, medical opinion, infection type-specific treatment guidelines, and anticipated prevalence of resistance at trial sites.
rules.verbatim_quote: The regimen selected should be considered one of the best available treatments based on clinical trials, medical opinion, infection type-specific treatment guidelines and the anticipated prevalence of resistance to the comparative agent(s) at the trial sites.
rules.exceptions: Use of an unlicensed antibacterial agent or dose regimen may sometimes be acceptable if adequately justified.

rules.rule_text: Pivotal efficacy trials should usually be double-blind. If a double-blind design is not feasible, an independent outcome adjudication committee blinded to treatment assignments should be used.
rules.verbatim_quote: Pivotal efficacy trials should usually be double-blind. If a double-blind design is not feasible every effort must be made to ensure that the physicians who assess clinical outcomes and report adverse events remain unaware of individual patient treatment assignments. In these settings, consideration should be given to use of an independent outcome adjudication committee that is blinded to treatment assignments.

rules.rule_text: Protocols should not require withdrawal from assigned therapy based on culture and susceptibility testing unless there is evidence of lack of improvement or the patient could be at significant risk if treatment is unchanged.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is generally recommended that protocols should not require that patients are withdrawn from assigned therapy based on culture and susceptibility testing unless there is evidence of lack of improvement or there are reasons to consider that the patient could be at significant risk if treatment is unchanged.

rules.rule_text: The TOC visit must occur within a pre-defined window of days after randomisation, selected so it occurs a minimum number of days after completion of therapy considering maximum treatment duration and elimination half-lives of test and comparator agents.
rules.verbatim_quote: The TOC visit should occur within a pre-defined window of days after randomisation. The window should be selected so that the TOC visit occurs at a minimum number of days after completion of therapy considering the maximum possible duration of active treatment allowed in the protocol and the elimination half-lives of the test and comparative antibacterial agents.

rules.rule_text: At the TOC visit, clinical outcome must be categorised as cure, failure, or indeterminate. The protocol must specify the criteria for determining cure.
rules.verbatim_quote: At the TOC visit the clinical outcome should be categorised as cure, failure or indeterminate. Cure may be defined as i) complete resolution of clinical signs and symptoms and/or ii) sufficient improvement or return to baseline status such that no further antibacterial therapy is required for the index infection. The protocol should specify the criteria that should be met to determine cure.

rules.rule_text: Documentation of the microbiological outcome is required when treating urinary tract infections and uncomplicated gonorrhoea.
rules.verbatim_quote: Documentation of the microbiological outcome is required when treating urinary tract infections and uncomplicated gonorrhoea.

rules.rule_text: For trials with a clinical primary endpoint, the primary analysis must be conducted in the all randomised (ITT) population. For trials with a microbiological or combined endpoint, the primary analysis must be in the microbiological-ITT population.
rules.verbatim_quote: In trials that have a clinical primary endpoint, the primary analysis should be conducted in the all randomised (ITT) population. In trials that have a microbiological primary endpoint or a combined clinical and microbiological response primary endpoint... the primary analysis should be conducted in the microbiological-ITT population

rules.rule_text: In non-inferiority trials, any patient with a baseline pathogen resistant to the comparative regimen must be removed from the primary analysis population before unblinding.
rules.verbatim_quote: In non-inferiority trials, any patient with any baseline pathogen that is resistant to the comparative regimen should be removed from the primary analysis population before unblinding the database to treatment assignment.

rules.rule_text: Clinical trial reports must include an integrated analysis of treatment failures, exploring whether individual and combinations of host, pathogen, and disease factors occur at higher rates in those who fail versus those who do not.
rules.verbatim_quote: Clinical trial reports should include an integrated analysis of treatment failures. These analyses should explore whether individual and combinations of host, pathogen and disease factors occur at higher rates in those who fail compared to those who do not fail.

rules.rule_text: An exposure-response analysis must be conducted. Predicted plasma exposures in those who do and do not fail should be compared, and any dose adjustments during the trials evaluated for appropriateness.
rules.verbatim_quote: An exposure-response analysis should be conducted as recommended in the Guideline on the use of pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics in the development of antimicrobial medicinal products (EMA/456046/2015). Also, predicted plasma exposures in those who do and do not fail should be viewed against any dose adjustments that were applied during the trials (e.g. for renal impairment) to evaluate whether these were appropriate.

rules.rule_text: A single pivotal trial may support an indication if combined with another infection site-specific trial meeting requirements of Section 6 (e.g. single trials in cUTI + uUTI, or CAP + HAP/VAP).
rules.verbatim_quote: Infection site-specific indications for use may be supported by single pivotal studies with standard levels of alpha (i.e. 2-sided 0.05) under certain circumstances. For example: ... Single trials in each of complicated urinary tract infections (cUTI) and uncomplicated urinary tract infection (uUTI); ... Single trials in each of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) and hospital acquired and/or ventilator-associated pneumonia (HAP and/or VAP)

rules.rule_text: For BL/BLI combinations where the BLI is unlicensed, at least one randomised controlled trial must be conducted in patients with one type of site-specific infection already approved for the BL alone, regardless of whether the BL/BLI addresses an unmet need.
rules.source_section: 5.7 Combination or co-administration of licensed beta-lactam agents with beta-lactamase inhibitors
rules.verbatim_quote: Regardless of whether the BL/BLI is expected to address an unmet need, it is recommended that at least one randomised controlled trial is conducted in patients with one type of site-specific infection already approved for the BL alone.

rules.rule_text: For ABSSSI trials, a non-inferiority margin of -10% for clinical outcome in the ITT population at the TOC visit must be used. Acceptable infection types include cellulitis, erysipelas, wound infections, and major abscesses.
rules.source_section: 6.1.1 Acute bacterial skin and skin structure infections (ABSSSI)
rules.verbatim_quote: Primary analysis: Clinical outcome in the ITT population at the TOC visit using a non-inferiority margin of -10%.
rules.exceptions: Patients with suspected/confirmed osteomyelitis, septic arthritis, or severe necrotising infections should be excluded.

rules.rule_text: For CAP trials, patients must have a chest radiograph within 48 hours showing new infiltrates, at least 3-4 of specified symptoms, and a minimum PORT score of III (with at least 25% >III) for IV treatment, or PORT II-III (with at least 50% at III) for oral treatment. Non-inferiority margin is -10%.
rules.verbatim_quote: Patient selection: A chest radiograph obtained within 48 hours prior to enrolment should show new infiltrates in a lobar or multilobar distribution... When treatment is to be initiated by the intravenous route patients should have a minimum PORT score of III and at least 25% should have a score >III... Primary analysis: Clinical outcome in the ITT population at the TOC visit using a non-inferiority margin of -10%.
rules.exceptions: Patients with cystic fibrosis, aspiration pneumonia, or specific obstruction should be excluded.

rules.rule_text: For HAP/VAP trials, in trials that include both HAP and VAP, approximately 30% of patients as a minimum should have VAP. Patients with VAP must have received mechanical ventilation for at least 48 hours. Non-inferiority margin is -12.5%.
rules.verbatim_quote: In trials that include patients with HAP or VAP, ~30% of patients as a minimum should have VAP... Patients with VAP should have received mechanical ventilation via an endotracheal or nasotracheal tube for at least 48 hours... Primary analysis: Clinical outcome in the ITT population at the TOC visit using a non-inferiority margin of -12.5%.

rules.rule_text: For cIAI trials, the primary analysis must use a non-inferiority margin of -10% in the microbiological-ITT population at TOC. Patients with appendicitis must not exceed 50% and stratification by appendix vs non-appendix cIAI is recommended.
rules.verbatim_quote: The proportion of patients with infections originating in the appendix should not exceed 50% and stratification at randomisation according to appendix and non-appendix associated cIAI is recommended... Primary analysis: Clinical outcome in the microbiological-ITT population at the TOC visit using a non-inferiority margin of -10%.
rules.exceptions: Patients with perforations of stomach/small intestine should not be enrolled unless there is evidence of established secondary infectious process.

rules.rule_text: For cUTI/AP trials, patients must have >1x10^5 CFU/mL of a single (or no more than two) relevant pathogens. If AP and cUTI are enrolled together, at least 30% must have cUTI and at least 30% must have AP. Non-inferiority margin is -10%.
rules.source_section: 6.1.5 Complicated urinary tract infections (cUTI) and acute pyelonephritis (AP)
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients eligible for the microbiological-ITT population should have > 1 x 10^5 CFU/mL of a single, or no more than two relevant pathogens in the baseline urine sample... it is recommended that at least 30% of the total enrolled should have cUTI and at least 30% should have AP... Combined clinical and microbiological (defined as < 1 x 10^3 CFU/mL in urine obtained at TOC visit) success rate... in the microbiological-ITT population at TOC using a non-inferiority margin of -10%.
rules.exceptions: Patients with ileal loops, vesico-ureteric reflux, or signs suggesting prostatitis should not be enrolled.

rules.rule_text: For uUTI trials, female patients with acute cystitis must have documented pyuria (>=10 WBCs/mm3) and >1x10^5 CFU/mL of a single relevant pathogen. Primary analysis uses combined clinical and microbiological success with NI margin of -10%.
rules.verbatim_quote: Patient selection: Female patients with acute cystitis should have a minimum number of symptoms such as frequency, urgency and dysuria. Patients may be enrolled before microbiological culture results are available based on documented pyuria (>= 10 WBCs/mm3)... Patients eligible for the microbiological-ITT population should have > 1 x 10^5 CFU/mL of a single relevant pathogen... Primary analysis: Combined clinical and microbiological success (defined as for cUTI) in the microbiological-ITT population at TOC using a non-inferiority margin of -10%.

rules.rule_text: For uncomplicated gonorrhoea trials, the primary analysis is microbiological eradication in the microbiological-ITT population at TOC using a NI margin of -10%. Patients must have a positive culture for N. gonorrhoeae.
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients eligible for the microbiological-ITT population should have a positive culture result for N. gonorrhoeae. Primary analysis: Microbiological eradication in the microbiological-ITT population at TOC using a non-inferiority margin of -10%.

rules.rule_text: For superficial skin infection trials, designs must show superiority over placebo. Separate trials must be conducted for specific types (superficial wound infections vs infected dermatoses) and for different dermatoses (e.g. infected atopic eczema vs infected psoriasis).
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, it is expected that trials will be designed to show superiority over a placebo. Separate trials should be conducted in specific types of infection, such as superficial wound infections and infected dermatoses. Moreover, due to differences in the pathogenesis and the treatment of various dermatoses, it is recommended that conditions such as infected atopic eczema and infected psoriasis should be studied in separate trials.

rules.rule_text: For pathogen-specific indications in patients with limited treatment options, in-vitro studies of a new class agent must demonstrate antibacterial activity unaffected by resistance to most or all relevant licensed antibacterial agents.
rules.verbatim_quote: For an antibacterial agent of a new class, in-vitro studies should demonstrate that its antibacterial activity is unaffected by resistance to most or all of the relevant licensed antibacterial agents.

rules.rule_text: PK-PD analyses are critically important for pathogen-specific indications to support that the clinical dose regimen is sufficient to treat the target multidrug-resistant organisms. PK data from infected patients enrolled in clinical trials must be used to update the population PK model and re-estimate PTA.
rules.source_section: 6.3.1 Establishing eligibility — PK considerations and PK-PD analyses
rules.verbatim_quote: The PK-PD analyses are critically important to support a conclusion that the clinical dose regimen is sufficient to treat the target multidrug-resistant organisms. It is essential that PK data from infected patients enrolled in clinical efficacy trials are used to update the population PK model and re-estimate the PTA to substantiate the adequacy of the proposed dose regimen in the application dossier.

rules.rule_text: For pathogen-specific indications, at least one randomised comparative trial must be conducted. When possible, each trial should be in a single infection type appropriate to the spectrum of activity.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is recommended that at least one randomised comparative trial is conducted. Whenever possible each trial should be conducted in a single type of infection that is appropriate to the spectrum of activity and PK of the test antibacterial product.
rules.exceptions: If spectrum is confined to uncommon or rare pathogens, patients with infections at different body sites may be enrolled.

rules.rule_text: For rare pathogens (e.g. anthrax, listeriosis) where clinical trials are not feasible, an indication for use may be based on in-vitro data, efficacy in nonclinical models, human PK data, and any relevant clinical experience.
rules.verbatim_quote: For rare pathogens (e.g. anthrax and listeriosis) for which it is not expected to be feasible to conduct a clinical trial, it may be possible to obtain an indication for use based on in-vitro data, efficacy in nonclinical models, human PK data and any relevant clinical experience

rules.rule_text: An indication for treatment of bacteraemia cannot be substantiated solely by a trial that enrols bacteraemic patients due to a specific pathogen regardless of primary focus of infection.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is not considered that an indication for treatment of bacteraemia can be substantiated solely by a trial that enrols patients with bacteraemia due to a specific pathogen regardless of the primary focus of infection, known or unknown.

rules.rule_text: For eradication of carriage trials, a primary endpoint based on reduction/eradication from a specified body site is not acceptable unless it has been soundly established that the microbiological effect results in a clinically important benefit such as reduction in post-procedure infection rates.
rules.verbatim_quote: A primary endpoint based on the reduction or eradication of a pathogen from a specified body site is not acceptable unless it has been soundly established that the microbiological effect results in a clinically important benefit, such as a reduction in the rate of post-procedure infections.

rules.rule_text: For C. difficile infection trials, the primary efficacy endpoint must be cure rate using a definition encompassing resolution of diarrhoea at a TOC visit timed at least 48 hours after the last dose. NI margin of -10% against a licensed agent in the ITT population. A late follow-up visit at approximately 40 days is required to document sustained cures and relapse rates.
rules.source_section: 6.5.3 Oral treatment to exert an action within the gut — C. difficile associated diarrhoea
rules.verbatim_quote: The primary efficacy endpoint should be the cure rate using a definition of cure that encompasses resolution of diarrhoea (using maximum number of stools per day and stool form criteria) at a TOC visit that should be timed to occur at least 48 hours after the last dose of study therapy... The primary analysis should demonstrate non-inferiority of the test agent compared to a licensed agent for cure rate at TOC in the ITT population using a non-inferiority margin of -10%. There should be a late follow-up visit at approximately 40 days from the day of randomisation to document sustained cures and early clinical relapse rates.

rules.rule_text: For prophylaxis trials where prophylaxis is not standard of care, a placebo-controlled trial is required to demonstrate superiority. Where prophylaxis is standard of care, a non-inferiority design against a licensed therapy is acceptable.
rules.verbatim_quote: If the role of prophylaxis has not been established and is not standard of care under the circumstances proposed for study, a placebo-controlled trial is required to demonstrate superiority of active treatment. If the role of antibacterial agents in preventing a specific type of infection in defined clinical circumstances is already established and is standard of care, a comparative study against a licensed therapy is acceptable if a non-inferiority margin can be justified

rules.rule_text: The SmPC Section 5.1 must include: ATC classification, mechanism of action, resistance information, antibacterial activity in combination, susceptibility testing interpretive criteria endorsed by CHMP/CMDh (in consultation with EUCAST), PK-PD relationship description, and clinical efficacy against specific pathogens demonstrated in clinical studies (at least 10 patients per species).
rules.source_section: 9 Summary of product characteristics — Section 5.1 Pharmacodynamic properties
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, at least 10 patients infected with a listed species or other acceptable grouping (e.g. A. baumannii complex) should have been treated with the test antibacterial agent and, as far as can be judged from small denominators, the results should not give cause for concern.
rules.exceptions: When the only indication is pathogen-specific in patients with limited treatment options, no list of 'pathogens successfully treated in clinical trials' should appear; instead pathogens should be listed under susceptibility sub-headings.
